over the past six years and what happened before you're doing that. see how quick I bridge to that next question? that's just a verbal cue. ah, and what happened before that? oh, you did, really? but did you guys actually do something before then? or did somebody actually say something to you? oh, you didn't actually do anything? but what did your boss say then? See how I'm just bridging from question to question. Using verbal cues like, 'ah,' or 'really,' or 'oh,' or 'hmm.' Okay, those are just little examples of that. So you want to master bridging from question to question. Because if you don't, your prospects, especially when you're in your situation, questions, which are more fact-finding, are going to feel like you're interrogating them.
They're going to feel like you're almost like an FBI agent and they're being interrogated. And after about three, four or five questions, it sounds scripted and surface-level. You're going to get pushback like, 'Hey, enough with the questions.' Can you just tell me more about what you're doing? I'll tell you if I'm interested. Or, you know, I appreciate the questions, but can you just get to the point and tell me how much it's going to cost? And I'll tell you if I'm interested. If you're ever getting that, it's because you're not bridging from question to question. You sound scripted, okay? So just like a Hollywood actor or actress. They, everything they pretty much say in the movies, almost 100% scripted, right? But does it sound scripted? Not at all, right?
Because they rehearse it. They know the lines. The body language, the tonality, the facial expressions. It's the same thing here. And once you understand the psychology behind each stage of any PQ, then you're going to be able to become flexible with your script, meaning that if you're in person and you've got some canned questions memorized, based off what they tell you from some of your questions, you'll know immediately. How to tweak the next questions on a dime, or if you're virtual or on the phone, right— you'll know. Like, they just answered it this way. So this next question I need to tweak it a little bit so it actually makes more sense to them, rather than just asking the next question on your script, because that sounds scripted.
Okay, let's go to reason number two. Okay, here's a big reason. The questions you're asking. Our service level questions. It's selling something you do to people or selling something you do for people. It's for people. Okay, so if you just ask vague questions, your prospects are going to give you very generalized, vague answers.
And you will never find out what the real situation is. And more importantly, they'll never really understand what the real situation is. And if you don't understand that, and they don't understand that, how are you going to build a gap to where they want to go or be? If they don't even know where they're at, okay?
